skill.name: auth-implementation-patterns
skill.description: Guides implementation of authentication and authorization patterns, including JWT, OAuth2, sessions, RBAC, permissions, resource ownership, password hashing, and rate limiting.
skill.summary: Build and review authentication and authorization flows with practical TypeScript and Express patterns.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: The static shell-execution, browser credential, browser storage, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, auth terminology, and best-practice text. The skill contains no executable script files, but one OAuth example redirects an access token in a URL query string, which is unsafe implementation guidance. Publication is acceptable with a warning or content correction.

medium_findings.title: OAuth Access Token Exposed in Redirect URL
medium_findings.description: The OAuth callback example redirects to the frontend with an access token in the query string. URL tokens can leak through browser history, logs, referrer headers, analytics, and support screenshots.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly builds a redirect URL containing token=${tokens.accessToken}. This is unsafe guidance, although it is documentation content rather than executable marketplace code.

low_findings.title: Environment Variables Used in Example Code
low_findings.description: The skill references secrets and connection strings through process.env in TypeScript examples. This is normal configuration guidance and not evidence of exfiltration, but users should avoid pasting real secrets into prompts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references are visible in documentation examples and are not read by executable skill code. The concern is limited to user handling of real secret values.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported shell execution, browser credential access, browser storage access, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns do not show malicious behavior in context. The evidence maps to Markdown fences, auth examples, and warnings such as avoiding JWT in localStorage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no shell command execution, browser credential file paths, weak cryptographic algorithms, or prompt injection text. The flagged text is explanatory Markdown and TypeScript sample code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Access Token in URL Query Parameter
dangerous_patterns.description: The OAuth sample passes an access token through a redirect query parameter. A safer pattern is an authorization code flow with PKCE, secure server exchange, and httpOnly cookies or secure token storage.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sample explicitly appends the access token to the callback URL. This is a well-known token leakage pattern.

content.value_statement: Authentication work is easy to get wrong and hard to review consistently. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for JWTs, sessions, OAuth2, RBAC, and API protection.
actual_capabilities: Explains authentication and authorization differences with practical examples.
Provides JWT generation, verification, refresh token, and middleware patterns.
Shows Express session setup with Redis-backed storage and secure cookie options.
Demonstrates OAuth2 social login integration with Passport.js examples.
Covers RBAC, permission checks, resource ownership, password hashing, and rate limiting.
Lists common authentication pitfalls and security review reminders.
limitations: Examples target TypeScript, Express, Passport.js, Redis, and similar Node.js stacks.
It is guidance content and does not run tests or verify a live application.
Some snippets are simplified and need adaptation before production use.
The OAuth redirect token example should be replaced with a safer flow before use.

use_cases.title: Design API Authentication
use_cases.description: Plan JWT middleware, refresh tokens, and protected routes for a new REST or GraphQL service.

use_cases.title: Review Authorization Rules
use_cases.description: Compare RBAC, permission checks, and resource ownership rules before adding protected operations.

use_cases.title: Modernize Login Flows
use_cases.description: Replace weak login handling with password hashing, rate limits, secure sessions, and OAuth2 guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an Auth Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to recommend an authentication pattern for my app. I have [users], [frontend], [backend], and [deployment model]. Explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Add JWT Middleware
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design JWT authentication middleware for this Express API. Include token lifetime, refresh flow, error handling, and route protection.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Authorization Logic
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my authorization model. Check RBAC, permissions, resource ownership, admin bypass rules, and missing server-side checks.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate Auth Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan a migration from session-only auth to OAuth2 plus first-party sessions. Include rollout phases, data changes, risks, and tests.

output_examples.input: I need secure login for an Express API.
output_examples.output: A recommended plan covering password hashing, short-lived access tokens, refresh token storage, authentication middleware, secure errors, and rate limiting.

output_examples.input: Review whether users can edit only their own posts.
output_examples.output: A review checklist for server-side ownership checks, admin exceptions, missing resource handling, forbidden responses, and route coverage.

output_examples.input: Add Google login to my app.
output_examples.output: A flow outline for OAuth2 configuration, callback handling, account linking, session creation, secure token handling, and failure cases.

best_practices: Use the examples as patterns, then adapt them to your framework, threat model, and compliance needs.
Keep real secrets out of prompts, issue trackers, screenshots, and generated examples.
Review token storage, cookie flags, session invalidation, and authorization checks before deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not copy simplified snippets into production without tests and security review.
Do not send access tokens through URLs, logs, analytics, or referrer-exposed redirects.
Do not rely on client-side authorization checks without server-side enforcement.

faq.question: Does this skill execute authentication code?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance and examples for an AI assistant to apply in your project.

faq.question: Which stack does it fit best?
faq.answer: It fits TypeScript and Express projects best, with examples using JWT, Passport.js, Redis, bcrypt, and rate limiting.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Does it replace a security audit?
faq.answer: No. It helps structure implementation and review, but production systems still need expert review and testing.

faq.question: Should I provide real environment variables?
faq.answer: No. Use placeholder names and never paste live secrets, keys, connection strings, or tokens into prompts.

faq.question: What should be changed before using the OAuth example?
faq.answer: Avoid placing access tokens in redirect URLs. Prefer authorization code with PKCE and secure server-side token handling.
